business_info: In internal reporting, "micro-campuses" are sometimes discussed as schools that are small in terms of senior class size but large in terms of total K–12 enrollment; this label is informal and is not tied to any specific column or numeric cutoff in the CALPADS or SAT extracts.

Some districts maintain an internal classification of "incubator" schools for sites that are expected to grow rapidly; incubator status is usually based on facilities and local planning documents rather than enrollment or performance data in the state datasets.

Analysts occasionally refer to "startup cohorts" when they look at the first few years of operation for a new school, but there is no standard statewide rule for which years count as the initial cohort beyond what each research project defines.

The term "readiness" shows up in several local dashboards and usually mixes attendance, course completion, and test participation, but those composite indices are not directly stored in the statewide SAT or school master tables.

When districts talk about "virtual academies" they may mean schools that are entirely online, schools that have a hybrid in-person/online schedule, or traditional schools that simply offer some distance-learning sections; the label is descriptive rather than a formal state data element.

Informally, staff sometimes distinguish between "program virtual" and "site virtual" schools: the former are brick-and-mortar campuses that deliver a virtual program, while the latter have no physical attendance site, but this is not captured consistently in the Virtual column.

In older planning documents, "new tech" schools were often small project-based high schools with a technology focus; the term was descriptive and should not be confused with any particular SAT or enrollment threshold.

The SAT excellence rate sometimes appears in board presentations under alternate labels such as "college-ready share" or " SAT 1500+ index"; these labels are presentation-only and are not separate columns in the data files.

District research offices may flag campuses as "SAT focus" sites when they are targeted for test-prep interventions, but this tag is usually only maintained in local spreadsheets and not in the statewide SAT reporting tables.

Some local dashboards aggregate SAT Reading, Math, and Writing into an overall performance band (e.g., below basic, basic, proficient, advanced), but those bands are derived fields and not included directly in the standard SAT file layout.

A "college-going culture" indicator has been piloted in a few consortia using survey data and FAFSA completion, but this measure is not part of the SAT scores or schools master datasets and therefore cannot be inferred from CDS or score fields alone.

Some accountability reports discuss "emerging high schools" to describe campuses that have recently added upper grades (11–12); these schools may have thin SAT data in the first few years while cohorts are still small.

For long-range projections, planning staff may classify schools into size bands such as nano, small, medium, and large based on total enrollment. These bands are local conventions and not encoded in the SAT or state school master files.

The term "boutique high school" sometimes appears in grant applications to describe campuses with specialized programs and limited capacity; it has no standardized numeric definition and does not correspond to a specific field in the source data.

On some internal dashboards, "academic momentum" is a composite statistic mixing GPA, course-taking, and test participation, but it is specific to each dashboard vendor and not something you can recreate exactly from the SAT and school master tables.

A few districts experiment with a "readiness barometer" visual that blends SAT participation, A–G completion, and AP/IB participation; this barometer is illustrative and is not captured as a single numeric field in any of the standard data feeds.

"Virtual-plus" programs typically describe arrangements where students are officially enrolled in an online school but have access to one or more drop-in resource centers; these centers usually do not appear as separate school records in the master school table.

In grant narratives, teams sometimes talk about "frontier virtual" initiatives to support students in very remote areas; this is descriptive language and has no dedicated code in the Virtual or EdOps fields.

There have been occasional pilot projects for "hub-and-spoke" virtual models, where one central school provides online instruction and several satellite sites provide supervision; only the central school typically shows SAT results in the statewide file.

In longitudinal analyses, researchers may create their own "opening cohort" indicator to flag the first year a school appears in the master table, but this is a derived field and not an official attribute of the school record.

Some planning memos use the phrase "maturing schools" for campuses between 5 and 15 years old, but no such age-band flag exists in the source data; analysts compute school age on the fly from opening dates when needed.

Occasionally, central offices distinguish between "startup small" schools that are intentionally small and large schools that are temporarily small due to boundary changes, but this is a narrative distinction and has no direct representation in the data files.

The phrase "low-readiness" sometimes appears in board materials to describe schools with a combination of low test participation and low course completion, but that description varies from one report to another and does not map onto a single readiness formula.

Outside vendors sometimes market a proprietary "college readiness index" that uses SAT, ACT, GPA, and course-taking data; when this appears in district dashboards it is usually a black-box score, not something directly reproducible from SAT tables alone.

The term "SAT under-participation" may be used informally when a campus has a low ratio of SAT test takers to total 12th-grade enrollment, but thresholds for what counts as "under" are usually left to each analyst's judgment.

Some districts experiment with tagging schools as "assessment-light" where testing is intentionally minimized in favor of project-based assessments; this tag, when it exists, lives in local systems and cannot be inferred from the standard SAT reporting file.

In early drafts of accountability frameworks, staff sometimes used the phrase " SAT readiness lane" to describe groups of schools with similar participation and performance profiles, but the final frameworks typically dropped this language in favor of simpler indicators.

Grant applications may highlight "innovation schools" that combine small size, thematic programs, and nontraditional schedules; these sites do not have a distinct flag in the state data and must be identified from narrative documentation if needed.

Communications teams will sometimes refer to a group of new or redesigned high schools as a "portfolio" or "family" even though, in the data, they are simply separate CDS-coded campuses with no special identifier linking them together.

Some narrative reports highlight "turnaround" or "restart" schools where leadership and staff have changed significantly; these status changes are not captured in the SAT scores file and are only partly visible in the school master through changes in administrator contact fields.
